// metric.rs.txt — standalone metric helpers for medial-axis golden tests.
//
// USAGE: `include!` this file inside a test module.
// It has NO dependency on the medial_axis implementation under test.
//
// Thresholds (verified against the golden derivation tolerances):
//   per_vertex_to_reference  ≤ 0.005 mm   — no spurious / off-axis output geometry
//   coverage_ref_to_polylines ≤ 0.005 mm  — full true axis is covered by output
//   max_width_error           ≤ 0.01  mm
//
// WHY THESE METRICS (not symmetric Hausdorff):
//   Symmetric point-set Hausdorff computes max(H(out→ref), H(ref→out)).
//   The H(ref→out) half measures each REFERENCE point against OUTPUT *points*.
//   When the output is a geometrically-exact but sparse polyline (e.g. a 2-point
//   straight spine) and the reference is a dense point cloud, most reference
//   points land far from any output point even though they are trivially close
//   to the output *segment*.  The result is a falsely-large metric that rejects a
//   correct implementation.  `coverage_ref_to_polylines` fixes this by measuring
//   reference points against output *segments* (point-to-segment distance), making
//   it friendly to sparse-but-exact output.  `per_vertex_to_reference` keeps the
//   output→reference direction (which is correct as-is) and renames it clearly.

// ─── POINT-TO-SEGMENT DISTANCE (private helper) ─────────────────────────────

/// Minimum distance from point `p` to the finite segment `(a, b)`.
fn point_to_segment_dist(p: (f64, f64), a: (f64, f64), b: (f64, f64)) -> f64 {
    let (px, py) = p;
    let (ax, ay) = a;
    let (bx, by) = b;
    let dx = bx - ax;
    let dy = by - ay;
    let len_sq = dx * dx + dy * dy;
    if len_sq == 0.0 {
        // Degenerate segment — treat as point
        let ex = px - ax;
        let ey = py - ay;
        return (ex * ex + ey * ey).sqrt();
    }
    // Project p onto the line through a–b, clamped to [0, 1]
    let t = ((px - ax) * dx + (py - ay) * dy) / len_sq;
    let t = t.clamp(0.0, 1.0);
    let cx = ax + t * dx;
    let cy = ay + t * dy;
    let ex = px - cx;
    let ey = py - cy;
    (ex * ex + ey * ey).sqrt()
}

// ─── METRIC 1: per-vertex output → reference ────────────────────────────────

/// Max over each OUTPUT vertex of its min Euclidean distance to the nearest
/// REFERENCE point.  The reference is typically a dense point cloud that
/// approximates the true medial axis, so this ≈ distance from output geometry
/// to the true axis.
///
/// Checks: "no spurious or off-axis output geometry."
///
/// Intended pass threshold: ≤ 0.005 mm (see `PER_VERTEX_PASS_MM`).
pub fn per_vertex_to_reference(out_pts: &[(f64, f64)], reference: &[(f64, f64)]) -> f64 {
    if out_pts.is_empty() || reference.is_empty() {
        return f64::INFINITY;
    }
    let mut max_min = 0.0_f64;
    for &(ox, oy) in out_pts {
        let min_d = reference
            .iter()
            .map(|&(rx, ry)| {
                let dx = ox - rx;
                let dy = oy - ry;
                (dx * dx + dy * dy).sqrt()
            })
            .fold(f64::INFINITY, f64::min);
        if min_d > max_min {
            max_min = min_d;
        }
    }
    max_min
}

// ─── METRIC 2: coverage reference → output polylines ────────────────────────

/// Max over each REFERENCE point of its min distance to the nearest OUTPUT
/// SEGMENT (point-to-segment distance over every consecutive vertex pair within
/// each output polyline).
///
/// Checks: "the whole true axis is covered by the output" while being
/// friendly to sparse-but-exact output (e.g. a 2-point straight spine whose
/// intermediate reference points are close to the *segment* but far from either
/// endpoint).
///
/// Intended pass threshold: ≤ 0.005 mm (see `COVERAGE_PASS_MM`).
pub fn coverage_ref_to_polylines(
    reference: &[(f64, f64)],
    polylines: &[Vec<(f64, f64)>],
) -> f64 {
    if reference.is_empty() || polylines.is_empty() {
        return f64::INFINITY;
    }
    let mut max_min = 0.0_f64;
    for &rp in reference {
        let min_d = polylines
            .iter()
            .flat_map(|pl| {
                // Iterate over consecutive segment pairs; also handle singleton polylines.
                if pl.len() < 2 {
                    // Single-point polyline — distance to that point.
                    let dist = if let Some(&pt) = pl.first() {
                        let dx = rp.0 - pt.0;
                        let dy = rp.1 - pt.1;
                        (dx * dx + dy * dy).sqrt()
                    } else {
                        f64::INFINITY
                    };
                    vec![dist]
                } else {
                    pl.windows(2)
                        .map(|w| point_to_segment_dist(rp, w[0], w[1]))
                        .collect()
                }
            })
            .fold(f64::INFINITY, f64::min);
        if min_d > max_min {
            max_min = min_d;
        }
    }
    max_min
}

// ─── METRIC 3: width error ───────────────────────────────────────────────────

/// For each output vertex `(x, y, width)`, find the nearest reference point by
/// xy distance and return the maximum `|out_width − ref_width|` across all
/// output vertices.
///
/// Inputs: slices of `(x, y, width)` tuples, all in mm.
/// Intended pass threshold: ≤ 0.01 mm (see `WIDTH_PASS_MM`).
pub fn max_width_error(out: &[(f64, f64, f64)], reference: &[(f64, f64, f64)]) -> f64 {
    if out.is_empty() || reference.is_empty() {
        return f64::INFINITY;
    }
    let mut max_err = 0.0_f64;
    for &(ox, oy, ow) in out {
        let (_, _, ref_w) = reference
            .iter()
            .copied()
            .min_by(|&(ax, ay, _), &(bx, by, _)| {
                let da = (ox - ax) * (ox - ax) + (oy - ay) * (oy - ay);
                let db = (ox - bx) * (ox - bx) + (oy - by) * (oy - by);
                da.partial_cmp(&db).unwrap_or(std::cmp::Ordering::Equal)
            })
            .unwrap();
        let err = (ow - ref_w).abs();
        if err > max_err {
            max_err = err;
        }
    }
    max_err
}

// ─── PASS THRESHOLDS ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
//
// PER_VERTEX_PASS_MM: per-vertex output→reference ≤ 0.005 mm
//   Rationale: the grid-derived goldens have resolution 0.002 mm; the closed-form
//   goldens are exact to floating-point precision. The implementation works in f32
//   (precision ~0.0001 mm at typical values). 0.005 mm gives 2.5× grid-resolution
//   headroom and ~50× floating-point headroom.
//
// COVERAGE_PASS_MM: coverage reference→polylines ≤ 0.005 mm
//   Rationale: same grid resolution; measured against output *segments* so
//   sparse-but-exact output is not penalised.
//
// WIDTH_PASS_MM: max width error ≤ 0.01 mm
//   Rationale: f32 width values at 1 mm scale have ~0.0001 mm precision; the golden
//   grid distance field has ~0.002 mm resolution; 0.01 mm is 5× the grid resolution.
//
pub const PER_VERTEX_PASS_MM: f64 = 0.005;
pub const COVERAGE_PASS_MM: f64 = 0.005;
pub const WIDTH_PASS_MM: f64 = 0.01;
